docs.title: Meridian
docs.text:
Meridian is a local-first CLI and GitHub Action for reviewing OpenTelemetry Collector configuration changes before merge.

It exists because Collector configuration is operationally important but hard to review with confidence. A config can be syntactically valid and still be wrong in ways that matter: unsupported components, broken routing, silent signal loss, or runtime behavior that reviewers cannot reconstruct after the fact.

Meridian addresses that gap by combining three evidence layers:

  1. repo-side structure and configuration analysis
  2. Collector-native semantic validation against a real distribution
  3. deterministic runtime execution against a patched test harness with durable artifacts

docs.title: Why teams use Meridian
docs.text:
Teams use Meridian when they need a review loop that answers practical engineering questions:

  • Does the config parse and wire correctly?
  • Does the selected Collector distribution actually accept it?
  • What changed, and how risky is that change?
  • Do traces, metrics, and logs still move through the intended pipelines?
  • Can a reviewer inspect evidence without rerunning the command?

docs.title: What Meridian proves
docs.text:
Meridian is designed to prove:

  • local configuration structure is materially sane when sources can be loaded repo-side
  • the selected Collector binary or image can validate the configuration
  • important pipeline changes are visible in graph and diff artifacts
  • synthetic telemetry can traverse the configured processing path in a deterministic runtime harness
  • assertions and contracts can be evaluated against captured output
  • every important run produces artifacts that remain useful in CI and local debugging

docs.title: What Meridian does not prove
docs.text:
Meridian does not try to replace production validation.

It does not prove:

  • real vendor backend authentication in the default safe path
  • live production ingestion health
  • generic Kubernetes compatibility outside the repo-owned fixture
  • any hosted service contract, because Meridian is intentionally local-first

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
New to Meridian

  • Read What Meridian Is
  • Follow Quickstart
  • Complete First Local Run

Reviewer or CI integrator

  • Read Evidence Model
  • Read GitHub Action
  • Follow Reviewer Pull Request Workflow

Maintainer or contributor

  • Read System Architecture
  • Read Package Map
  • Read Contributing

docs.title: Recommended learning path
docs.text:
1. Understand the product intent in Overview.
  2. Get a successful run in Getting Started.
  3. Build the right mental model in Core Concepts.
  4. Deepen feature knowledge in Feature Deep Dives.
  5. Adopt a real operating pattern in Recommended Workflows.
  6. Study implementation details in Architecture.

docs.title: Feature map
docs.text: Need Meridian feature Validate static config correctness validate Visualize pipeline wiring graph Review risk between revisions diff Prove runtime flow in a safe harness test, check, and ci Lock behavior with output expectations Assertions and contracts Inspect run evidence after the fact Debug commands Put the same checks in pull requests GitHub Action Regression test Meridian itself in Kubernetes K3s fixture

docs.title: Workflow at a glance
docs.text:
The recommended path for a config author is:

author config change
  -> meridian validate
  -> meridian check
  -> inspect summary.md / report.json / artifacts
  -> open PR
  -> CI runs meridian ci through the GitHub Action
  -> reviewer inspects PR comment, step summary, and artifact bundle

Meridian is most useful when its artifacts are treated as review evidence, not only as a pass or fail bit.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
This section explains how Meridian is implemented today.

It is written for engineers who need to understand the system, maintain it, or reason about why the docs describe a feature the way they do.

docs.title: Read this section if you need to understand
docs.text:
• the end-to-end runtime pipeline
  • package responsibilities and seams
  • how reports and artifacts are produced
  • how CI integration and docs publishing fit together

docs.title: Suggested order
docs.text:
1. System Architecture
  2. Execution Pipeline
  3. Package Map
  4. Reporting and Artifacts
  5. CI and Docs Publishing

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Meridian has two distinct but related automation surfaces:

  • the product-facing GitHub Action for validating Collector configs
  • the repository's own docs publishing workflow

docs.title: Product CI path
docs.text:
The composite action:

  • builds the Meridian binary
  • runs meridian ci
  • uploads artifacts
  • writes the step summary
  • optionally updates a PR comment

This is the workflow external repositories consume.

docs.title: Docs publishing path
docs.text:
The docs workflow:

  • regenerates CLI reference pages
  • fails on generated-doc drift
  • builds the MkDocs site in strict mode
  • deploys dev from main
  • deploys release docs on v* tags using mike

docs.title: Why it is separate
docs.text:
Docs publishing and product CI solve different problems:

  • product CI validates Collector configs
  • docs CI validates and publishes Meridian documentation

Keeping them separate prevents documentation tooling from leaking into the normal product validation surface.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The runtime path is centered on RunService in internal/app/run.go. This is the best place to understand the real operational sequence for test, check, and ci.

docs.title: 1. Load inputs
docs.text: Meridian validates global and runtime options, expands config sources, loads env values, and attempts repo-side config loading.

docs.title: 2. Prepare artifacts and engine
docs.text: It allocates a run directory, reserves runtime ports, and resolves the selected runtime engine.

docs.title: 3. Run semantic validation
docs.text: It validates the config against the selected Collector target and records stage results and findings.

docs.title: 4. Select the runtime config
docs.text:
It chooses the runtime config source:

  • repo-side merged config when available
  • Collector-rendered config when required and available

docs.title: 5. Write static artifacts
docs.text: It runs repo-side validation and graph generation on the runtime config model and stores those results in the pending run result.

docs.title: 6. Patch the runtime config
docs.text: It injects the Meridian receiver/exporter pair, adjusts selected pipelines, and builds a TestPlan.

docs.title: 7. Attach diff analysis
docs.text: When diff inputs are present or the command includes diff-aware behavior, it attaches diff results to the run.

docs.title: 8. Execute the runtime harness
docs.text: The runtime runner starts the Collector, injects synthetic telemetry, waits for capture, persists normalized capture output, evaluates assertions and contracts, and collects logs.

docs.title: 9. Write the bundle
docs.text: Reporting writes report.json, summary.md, diff and contract artifacts, semantic artifacts, and the latest symlink.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: This explicit pipeline is the backbone of both the docs and the codebase. When describing a feature, the docs should make it clear where that feature sits in the sequence.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian's package boundaries reflect operational responsibilities rather than theoretical layers.

docs.title: internal/app
docs.text: Builds the Cobra command tree, validates options, and orchestrates high-level command behavior.

docs.title: internal/configio
docs.text: Expands config inputs, materializes local sources, interpolates environment values, and produces normalized config models.

docs.title: internal/collector
docs.text: Executes Collector-native semantic validation and effective-config retrieval using either a local binary or an image through the selected engine.

docs.title: internal/graph
docs.text: Builds the graph model that powers Mermaid, DOT, SVG, and table output.

docs.title: internal/diffing
docs.text: Compares old and new configurations and classifies review-relevant changes.

docs.title: internal/patch
docs.text: Patches the config for runtime execution and emits the TestPlan.

docs.title: internal/runtime
docs.text: Owns engine resolution, container startup, capture sink wiring, telemetry injection, capture waiting, and container cleanup.

docs.title: internal/assert
docs.text: Loads assertion and contract suites and evaluates them against captured or normalized telemetry.

docs.title: internal/report
docs.text: Renders human-facing summaries, diff markdown, contract markdown, terminal output, PR comments, and persists bundle artifacts.

docs.title: internal/model
docs.text: Defines shared types such as config models, run results, semantic reports, artifact manifests, and contract structures.

docs.title: Architectural implication
docs.text: The docs should mirror these boundaries. If a documentation page spans too many of these responsibilities without naming them, it will usually feel vague.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian's reporting subsystem turns runtime state into durable evidence. This matters because the human product surface is often the artifact bundle, not the original terminal session.

docs.title: How bundle writing works
docs.text:
report.WriteBundle is responsible for:

  • ensuring the run directory exists
  • writing semantic findings and component inventory when available
  • writing config.final.yaml when effective config exists
  • writing report.json
  • writing summary.md
  • writing diff.md when diff data exists
  • writing contracts.json and contracts.md
  • updating the runs/latest symlink

docs.title: Summary rendering
docs.text:
RenderSummaryMarkdown builds the reviewer-facing markdown summary. It focuses on:

  • overall run state
  • semantic stage status
  • risk highlights from diff analysis
  • contract and assertion results
  • top failure context
  • artifact names

docs.title: CI-facing reporting
docs.text:
The same report subsystem also provides:

  • PR comment markdown
  • terminal summaries
  • GitHub workflow command annotations for failures

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: When the docs describe “what a run produced,” they should align with the actual bundle writer and renderer behavior rather than an idealized artifact list.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian is organized as a Go CLI with explicit subsystems for config loading, semantic validation, graphing, diffing, patching, runtime execution, assertion evaluation, and reporting.

docs.title: End-to-end view
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A["Config Inputs (--config / --config-dir / URIs)"] --> B["configio: expand, materialize, interpolate"]
    B --> C["validate: repo-side findings"]
    B --> D["collector: semantic validation and print-config"]
    C --> E["graph / diff preparation"]
    D --> F["runtime config selection"]
    F --> G["patch: inject receiver/exporter and build test plan"]
    G --> H["runtime: start collector, inject telemetry, wait for capture"]
    H --> I["assert: default assertions and contracts"]
    I --> J["report: report.json, summary.md, diff.md, contracts.md"]
    J --> K["debug commands / CI summary / PR comment / uploaded artifacts"]

docs.title: Major subsystems
docs.text:
• internal/app: command definitions, option parsing, and orchestration entry points
  • internal/configio: source expansion, materialization, YAML loading, env interpolation
  • internal/collector: Collector-native semantic validation and effective-config resolution
  • internal/graph: pipeline graph model generation
  • internal/diffing: structural diff classification
  • internal/patch: runtime config patching and test-plan generation
  • internal/runtime: container execution, injection, capture wait, runtime adapters
  • internal/assert: assertions, contract loading, and evaluation
  • internal/report: markdown, terminal, diff, contract, and bundle rendering
  • internal/model: shared types and artifact manifest definitions

docs.title: Architectural intent
docs.text: Meridian is intentionally concrete. The implementation favors explicit stages over framework-heavy abstractions so that a failing run can usually be traced to one subsystem and one stage.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Meridian is easiest to use once the underlying mental model is clear. This section explains how the product thinks about evidence, configuration sources, runtime patching, engines, and artifacts.

docs.title: Read this section if you need to understand
docs.text:
• what a Meridian result actually means
  • why some config inputs can be parsed locally and others cannot
  • why safe mode exists and how it differs from tee and live
  • how engine resolution affects both semantic validation and runtime execution
  • why artifact provenance matters in review

docs.title: Recommended order
docs.text:
1. Evidence Model
  2. Config Source Model
  3. Semantic Validation
  4. Runtime Modes and Patching
  5. Runtime Engine Model
  6. Artifact Model

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Artifacts are a first-class output of Meridian. They are not secondary debug files; they are the product surface reviewers use to understand what happened.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
Runtime-oriented runs use:

meridian-artifacts/
  runs/
    latest -> <run_id>
    <run_id>/

The latest symlink is resolved relative to the selected --output root and powers the default behavior of the debug commands.

docs.title: Core artifacts
docs.text:
The baseline runtime bundle includes:

  • report.json
  • summary.md
  • config.patched.yaml
  • graph.mmd
  • collector.log
  • captures/

Additional artifacts appear when that evidence exists:

  • config.final.yaml
  • collector-components.json
  • semantic-findings.json
  • graph.svg
  • diff.md
  • contracts.json
  • contracts.md
  • capture.normalized.json

docs.title: Provenance and interpretation
docs.text:
The bundle is more useful when engineers distinguish:

  • original config sources
  • runtime config source
  • patched execution config

That distinction matters when effective config is derived through Collector print-config rather than repo-local YAML materialization.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Debug Commands
  • Artifact Contract

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian accepts more than one kind of Collector input, but not all inputs are equally inspectable. The config source model explains when Meridian can reason about config locally and when it must rely on Collector-native behavior.

docs.title: Supported input shapes
docs.text:
Meridian accepts:

  • repeatable --config values
  • --config-dir
  • local file paths
  • supported Collector-native config URIs

Examples include local YAML files and URI-style inputs such as yaml: and file:.

docs.title: How local loading works
docs.text:
The configio package expands the source list, materializes any locally materializable sources, interpolates env values, merges YAML documents, and produces a normalized config model.

Repo-side parsing depends on at least one materializable YAML source. If all inputs are URI-only and cannot be materialized locally, Meridian reports that repo-side parsing was skipped.

docs.title: Why this matters at runtime
docs.text:
Runtime patching needs a concrete config model. Meridian can get that in two ways:

  • from source-merged config when all inputs are locally materializable
  • from Collector print-config when URI sources require Collector-native rendering

If neither path is available, runtime preparation fails before the Collector starts.

docs.title: Environment interpolation
docs.text:
Meridian resolves env values from:

  1. --env-file
  2. repeatable --env KEY=value
  3. exported shell environment variables

Missing env values are tracked as part of the config model and should be treated as review-relevant evidence, not just a parsing nuisance.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Config Inputs reference
  • Semantic Validation

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian is an evidence-producing tool. The right way to evaluate it is not “did the command run” but “what evidence did the run produce, and what does that evidence justify?”

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text:
Collector review often collapses into intuition because the available signals are weak:

  • YAML looks plausible
  • the config validates somewhere else
  • a reviewer assumes runtime behavior stayed intact

Meridian replaces that with layered evidence.

docs.title: Repo-side evidence
docs.text: Repo-side loading and validation operate on locally materializable inputs. This layer can catch structural problems, environment interpolation issues, and graph-visible topology shape.

docs.title: Collector-native evidence
docs.text: Semantic validation asks a real Collector target to list components, validate the config, and best-effort render effective config with print-config.

docs.title: Runtime evidence
docs.text: Runtime execution uses a patched harness, injects synthetic telemetry, captures outputs, and evaluates assertions or contracts against those captures.

docs.title: What Meridian proves and does not prove
docs.text:
Meridian proves that the selected inputs and target produced the observed evidence under the selected mode and engine. It does not prove production correctness beyond that scope.

The trust boundary is especially important in safe mode:

  • strong evidence for flow and reviewability
  • intentionally limited evidence for live backend behavior

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Semantic Validation
  • Runtime Modes and Patching

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian treats the container engine as a first-class runtime input because it affects both semantic validation and runtime execution.

docs.title: Supported engines
docs.text:
• auto
  • docker
  • containerd

docs.title: Resolution rules
docs.text: auto prefers Docker when both Docker and containerd paths are available. This preserves a stable happy path for common local and CI environments.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text: Docker is the default expectation for local runs and GitHub Actions. For capture routing, Meridian uses a host alias strategy.

docs.title: Linux containerd
docs.text: Linux containerd expects nerdctl and uses host networking for the capture path.

docs.title: macOS containerd
docs.text: macOS containerd uses lima nerdctl, published injection ports, and host.lima.internal for the capture sink endpoint.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Engine selection affects:

  • how Collector images are run for semantic validation
  • how runtime ports are wired
  • what repro steps are realistic
  • what troubleshooting commands make sense

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Runtime Commands
  • Recommended Workflows

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Runtime mode defines how Meridian rewrites the target config for execution. This is the heart of the product's deterministic review model.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: A normal Collector config may send data to live backends or external services. That is often the wrong first feedback loop for pre-merge review. Meridian instead patches the config so it can inject and capture telemetry in a controlled way.

docs.title: How patching works
docs.text:
The patch package:

  • injects a Meridian-managed OTLP receiver
  • injects a Meridian-managed OTLP capture exporter
  • selects pipelines by name or signal when requested
  • rewrites pipeline receivers and exporters according to the chosen mode
  • preserves connector exporters in safe mode
  • records the resulting plan and writes config.patched.yaml

docs.title: safe
docs.text:
safe replaces normal exporters with the Meridian capture exporter while preserving connector exporters.

Use this when you want deterministic, low-risk evidence.

docs.title: tee
docs.text:
tee preserves original exporters and appends Meridian capture.

Use it when you want to observe runtime output while keeping the original exporter path active.

docs.title: live
docs.text:
live also preserves real exporters and appends Meridian capture.

Use it only when synthetic telemetry reaching real destinations is acceptable.

docs.title: What it proves and does not prove
docs.text:
safe proves pipeline flow under the patched harness. It does not prove live vendor connectivity.

tee and live increase realism, but they also increase operational risk and reduce the purity of the deterministic test harness.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Runtime Commands
  • Artifact Model

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Semantic validation is Meridian's bridge between static YAML analysis and actual Collector behavior. It validates the selected config against a real Collector target before runtime execution.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text:
Repo-side parsing can tell you whether the config is structurally understandable. It cannot tell you whether the Collector distribution you intend to run:

  • supports the referenced components
  • accepts the actual config schema
  • can render a usable effective config

Semantic validation closes that gap.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Meridian resolves the semantic target in this order:

  1. --collector-binary
  2. --collector-image through the selected engine
  3. skip semantic validation where the command allows it

It then executes these stages:

  • components
  • validate
  • print-config

print-config is best effort and requires the otelcol.printInitialConfig feature gate.

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text:
Semantic validation proves that the selected Collector target can execute the relevant validation stages for the provided config sources.

It does not prove runtime flow by itself. That remains the job of the runtime harness.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text:
Key inputs:

  • config sources
  • env values
  • Collector binary or image
  • engine selection

Key outputs:

  • stage results in summary.md and report.json
  • collector-components.json when inventory is available
  • semantic-findings.json when findings exist
  • config.final.yaml when print-config succeeds

docs.title: Failure modes and limits
docs.text:
Expect degraded behavior when:

  • no semantic target can be resolved
  • the selected Collector does not implement components
  • print-config is unsupported or unavailable
  • the target Collector rejects the config

Meridian reports those cases explicitly so effective-config evidence is never implied when it does not exist.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Validate
  • Artifact Model

docs.title: Feature Deep Dives
docs.text:
This section explains Meridian capability by capability. Each page is written to answer the same engineering questions:

  • what problem this feature solves
  • how it actually works
  • what it proves and does not prove
  • what artifacts or outputs it produces
  • where it fits in a real workflow

docs.title: Read in this order if you are new
docs.text:
1. Validate
  2. Runtime Commands
  3. Assertions and Contracts
  4. Diff
  5. Debug Commands
  6. GitHub Action

docs.title: Read selectively if you already use Meridian
docs.text:
• topology concerns: Graph
  • review-risk concerns: Diff
  • CI concerns: GitHub Action
  • maintainer concerns: K3s Fixture

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Meridian supports two layers of output validation:

  • v1 assertions for runtime flow checks
  • v2 contracts for normalized, reviewer-facing behavior checks

docs.title: Why they exist
docs.text: A passing runtime run only tells you that telemetry moved. It does not necessarily tell you that the output shape is still correct after filtering, routing, or mutation. Assertions and contracts raise the review bar.

docs.title: How assertions work
docs.text:
Meridian always applies default assertions for each active signal:

  • at least one item was received
  • telemetry arrived within the configured capture timeout
  • the run ID is present
  • the capture sink decoded the payload without errors

Custom v1 assertions extend that with filters and expectations.

docs.title: How contracts work
docs.text: Contracts operate on normalized telemetry and are intended for reviewer confidence after transformations. They support fixture-aware evaluation and richer expectations such as exact counts, string matching, regex, and numeric metric checks.

docs.title: What they prove
docs.text:
Assertions prove runtime-flow conditions over captured telemetry.

Contracts prove that the normalized output satisfies the declared expectations for the selected fixture and signal.

Neither should be relaxed casually. They are part of the review contract.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
Contract-related artifacts include:

  • contracts.json
  • contracts.md
  • capture.normalized.json

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Runtime Commands
  • Examples

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The debug command group is Meridian's post-run inspection surface. It exists because engineers and reviewers often consume output after the original command already finished.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: Meridian is artifact-first. The debug commands make those artifacts easier to consume without asking a user to remember bundle paths manually.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• debug summary: print the stored markdown summary
  • debug bundle: print the bundle manifest in human or JSON form
  • debug logs: print stored Collector logs
  • debug capture: print persisted capture samples

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: When --run is omitted, debug commands resolve runs/latest relative to the selected output root. This keeps the common local inspection path simple.

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text: Debug commands do not create new evidence. They expose previously generated evidence in a useful form.

docs.title: Failure modes
docs.text:
Failures usually mean:

  • the expected run directory does not exist
  • latest points to a missing path
  • the artifact was never generated because the run failed earlier

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Artifact Model
  • Artifact Contract

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: meridian diff compares two Collector configurations and classifies review-relevant changes. It is designed to highlight risk, not to replace a generic text diff.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: Not every config edit deserves the same scrutiny. Reviewers care most about changes that affect routing, processors, connectors, extensions, auth, TLS, and telemetry service behavior.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Meridian can diff:

  • explicit old and new file paths
  • git base and head refs

When effective config from Collector print-config is available, Meridian prefers that evidence. Otherwise it falls back to source-level comparison.

Runtime commands can also incorporate diff-aware review hints and optionally restrict them with --changed-only.

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text: Diff output proves how Meridian classified the structural change between two configurations. It does not prove that the changed config is safe by itself; use it together with validation and runtime evidence.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text:
Important flags:

  • --old
  • --new
  • --base
  • --head
  • --severity-threshold
  • --changed-only in runtime flows

Artifacts:

  • diff section in report.json
  • highlights in summary.md
  • diff.md when diff data exists

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Graph
  • Reviewer Pull Request Workflow

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The composite GitHub Action makes Meridian practical in pull requests by standardizing how meridian ci is executed and how its evidence is surfaced.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text:
Teams need more than a job that returns exit code 0 or 1. They need CI feedback that is:

  • reviewable
  • durable
  • consistent across pull requests

The action provides that wrapper without hiding the underlying CLI behavior.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The action:

  1. builds the Meridian binary from the checked-out repository
  2. runs meridian ci
  3. uploads artifacts
  4. writes the GitHub step summary
  5. updates a single PR comment when comment mode is enabled

It accepts the same runtime selector as the CLI: auto, docker, or containerd.

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text: The action proves whatever meridian ci proves for the selected inputs. It does not add extra validation semantics beyond packaging those results for GitHub review.

docs.title: Limits
docs.text:
• containerd is intended for Linux runners with nerdctl
  • the current implementation depends on actions/upload-artifact@v4
  • GHES compatibility is not part of the current contract

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• GitHub Action Reference
  • CI Maintainer Workflow

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: meridian graph turns the Collector config into a graph model so engineers can review pipeline wiring directly rather than infer it from nested YAML.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text:
Topology changes are hard to review in raw configuration. Reviewers need to see:

  • which receivers feed which pipelines
  • which processors sit in the path
  • where exporters and connectors sit
  • whether the shape of the configuration changed materially

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Meridian builds a graph model from the normalized config representation and renders:

  • Mermaid by default
  • DOT or SVG when requested
  • a table view in terminal output when --view table is used

Graph artifacts are also generated during runtime-oriented commands when requested.

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text: Graph output proves how Meridian interpreted the config model. It is a review aid, not a runtime guarantee.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text:
Important flags:

  • --render mermaid|dot|svg|none
  • --view table
  • --out

Typical outputs:

  • stdout graph view
  • graph.mmd
  • optional graph.svg

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Diff
  • Artifact Model

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The repository-owned fixture under examples/k3s-e2e/ is Meridian's real-cluster regression target for Meridian itself.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: Meridian needs one authoritative cluster-level validation environment that the project owns end to end. Using a repo-owned fixture keeps the acceptance surface small, editable, and understandable.

docs.title: What it includes
docs.text:
The fixture uses four application roles:

  • storefront
  • checkout
  • inventory
  • trafficgen

These services are intentionally limited so regressions can be diagnosed from artifacts instead of from a large demo stack.

docs.title: Scenario model
docs.text:
Scenarios include:

  • happy
  • drop-traces
  • misroute-logs
  • auth-fail
  • backend-unreachable

Negative scenarios are expected to pass when the expected failure mode is detected correctly.

docs.title: Acceptance signals
docs.text:
Primary signals on the project VM are:

  • pod logs
  • Prometheus evidence
  • gateway counters

Tempo and Loki remain supporting evidence, but are not the sole blocking signal in every scenario because they are not always fully run-scoped.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Meridian Maintainer Regression Workflow
  • Artifact Contract

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The runtime commands are Meridian's core confidence path. They run a deterministic harness against a patched Collector config and write a bundle of evidence.

docs.title: Why they exist
docs.text: Static validation can tell you whether a config is understandable. It cannot prove that telemetry still traverses the intended pipelines. The runtime commands exist to answer that question in a controlled environment.

docs.title: test
docs.text: Runs the runtime harness directly. Use it when you want focused local execution without the opinionated review framing of check.

docs.title: check
docs.text: Runs Meridian's opinionated end-to-end confidence workflow. This is the recommended local command for most engineers.

docs.title: ci
docs.text: Wraps the same workflow in a CI-oriented interface, with machine-readable output, step summary writing, PR comment rendering, and annotation behavior.

docs.title: How they work
docs.text:
At a high level, Meridian:

  1. loads and validates inputs
  2. runs semantic validation
  3. selects the runtime config source
  4. writes static artifacts such as graph output
  5. patches the config
  6. optionally attaches diff analysis
  7. starts the Collector through the selected engine
  8. injects synthetic telemetry
  9. waits for capture
  10. evaluates assertions and contracts
  11. writes the bundle and summary

docs.title: What they prove and do not prove
docs.text:
In safe mode, these commands prove deterministic pipeline flow through the patched harness. They do not prove real backend behavior.

tee and live can provide more realistic destination behavior, but they change the risk profile and should not be treated as equally deterministic.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text:
Important flags include:

  • --mode
  • --engine
  • --collector-image
  • --assertions
  • --pipelines
  • --render-graph
  • --output
  • --keep-containers

ci adds:

  • --summary-file
  • --json-file
  • --pr-comment-file
  • --pr

docs.title: Failure modes
docs.text:
Common failure sources:

  • runtime engine preflight failure
  • Collector startup failure
  • telemetry injection failure
  • no capture for the expected signal
  • failing assertions or contracts

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Runtime Modes and Patching
  • Execution Pipeline

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: meridian validate is the fast, static-confidence entry point. It combines repo-side validation with Collector-native semantic validation when a binary or image target is available.

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: Collector review needs a fast first pass that is stronger than YAML parsing but cheaper than runtime execution. validate is that layer.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
validate:

  1. expands config sources
  2. attempts repo-side loading through configio
  3. reports when local loading is skipped for URI-only inputs
  4. loads environment values
  5. runs Collector-native semantic validation through collector.Analyze
  6. prints a human summary or JSON payload

docs.title: What it proves
docs.text:
It proves that Meridian can understand the config locally when materialization is possible and that the selected Collector target can validate the config at the semantic layer.

It does not prove runtime flow.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text:
Primary inputs:

  • --config
  • --config-dir
  • --env-file
  • --env
  • --collector-binary
  • --collector-image
  • --engine

Primary outputs:

  • terminal or JSON stage summary
  • findings grouped by severity

docs.title: Failure modes
docs.text:
Common failure cases:

  • no config input was provided
  • env interpolation failed
  • local loading failed on malformed YAML
  • semantic target could not be resolved
  • Collector-native validation failed

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Semantic Validation
  • Config Inputs

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This section is the shortest path from zero context to a trustworthy first result.

docs.title: Recommended path
docs.text:
1. Install
  2. Quickstart
  3. First Local Run
  4. First CI Setup

docs.title: What success looks like
docs.text:
After completing this section, you should be able to:

  • build or install the Meridian binary
  • run validate and understand its output
  • run check in safe mode and inspect the resulting artifacts
  • connect the same workflow to GitHub Actions for pull request feedback

docs.title: Before you continue
docs.text: If you are evaluating Meridian for a team, it is worth reading Evidence Model after the quickstart. That page explains what Meridian's results mean and where their trust boundary ends.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The goal of a first CI setup is parity with the local ci workflow, not a custom wrapper around Meridian.

docs.title: Minimal workflow
docs.text:
name: meridian

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "otel/**"
      - "**/collector*.yaml"
      - "**/otelcol*.yaml"

jobs:
  meridian:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: salman-frs/meridian/action@v1
        with:
          config: examples/basic/collector.yaml
          engine: auto

docs.title: What this produces
docs.text:
The composite action:

  • builds the Meridian binary
  • runs meridian ci
  • uploads artifacts
  • writes the GitHub step summary
  • optionally updates a single PR comment marked with <!-- meridian-comment -->

docs.title: Recommended review behavior
docs.text:
Teach reviewers to inspect:

  • the step summary for a quick read
  • the PR comment for persistent status
  • the artifact bundle for full evidence

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• GitHub Action
  • Reviewer Pull Request Workflow

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: A useful first local run is not just executing a command. It is understanding what happened and where the evidence lives.

docs.title: Build the binary
docs.text: go build -o ./bin/meridian ./cmd/meridian

docs.title: Validate a known-good config
docs.text: ./bin/meridian validate -c examples/basic/collector.yaml

docs.title: Run the default confidence path
docs.text: ./bin/meridian check -c examples/basic/collector.yaml

docs.title: Inspect the results
docs.text:
./bin/meridian debug summary
./bin/meridian debug bundle

docs.title: What to verify
docs.text:
After your first run, confirm that you can identify:

  • which config source Meridian used
  • which semantic target it selected
  • whether runtime patching used repo-local config or Collector-rendered config
  • where summary.md, report.json, collector.log, and config.patched.yaml live
  • how runs/latest resolves

docs.title: If env values are involved
docs.text:
Use:

./bin/meridian validate -c collector.yaml --env-file .env --env OTLP_ENDPOINT=localhost:4317

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Config Source Model
  • Artifact Model

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Meridian is a single Go binary. Most installations are either:

  • a local source build
  • a release binary download

The runtime and graph features have additional dependencies depending on what commands you plan to use.

docs.title: Build from source
docs.text:
go build -o ./bin/meridian ./cmd/meridian

Requirements:

  • Go 1.25 or newer
  • a supported container engine for runtime commands

docs.title: Install a release binary
docs.text:
curl -fsSL -o meridian.tar.gz https://github.com/salman-frs/meridian/releases/latest/download/meridian-linux-amd64.tar.gz
tar -xzf meridian.tar.gz
./meridian version

docs.title: Runtime prerequisites
docs.text: Capability Requirement Repo-side validation only local files and the Meridian binary Semantic validation via image Docker or containerd Runtime commands Docker, or nerdctl, or lima nerdctl on macOS SVG graph rendering Graphviz dot

docs.title: Engine-specific notes
docs.text:
• auto prefers Docker when both Docker and containerd are available
  • Linux containerd expects nerdctl
  • macOS containerd uses lima nerdctl

docs.title: Docs development prerequisites
docs.text:
If you are contributing to the docs site:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install -r docs/requirements.txt
go run ./cmd/meridian-docs
mkdocs serve

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Quickstart
  • Runtime Engine Model

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This quickstart is intentionally narrow. The goal is one successful local validation and one successful runtime-backed check.

docs.title: 1. Build Meridian
docs.text: go build -o ./bin/meridian ./cmd/meridian

docs.title: 2. Run static validation
docs.text:
./bin/meridian validate -c examples/basic/collector.yaml

This proves that Meridian can:

  • resolve the config input
  • load local YAML when applicable
  • run repo-side validation
  • attempt Collector-native semantic validation

docs.title: 3. Run the opinionated confidence workflow
docs.text:
./bin/meridian check -c examples/basic/collector.yaml

This is the shortest path to a complete Meridian run. It will produce:

  • summary.md
  • report.json
  • config.patched.yaml
  • graph.mmd
  • collector logs
  • capture artifacts

Additional semantic, diff, and contract artifacts appear when the run produces them.

docs.title: 4. Inspect the latest bundle
docs.text:
./bin/meridian debug summary
./bin/meridian debug bundle --format json
./bin/meridian debug logs
./bin/meridian debug capture

docs.title: What to pay attention to
docs.text:
Do not treat a passing run as a single green light. Look at:

  • semantic stage results
  • graph and diff evidence when reviewing change
  • runtime status by signal
  • any contract failures or warnings

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• First Local Run
  • Runtime Commands

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Meridian is an engineer-first tool for proving OpenTelemetry Collector changes before merge. It is not a linter with a nicer UI and it is not a hosted validation service. It is a pragmatic CLI and GitHub Action that produces evidence a reviewer can trust.

docs.title: What Meridian is
docs.text:
At a high level, Meridian does four things:

  • reads Collector configuration from local files, rendered directories, and supported Collector-native config URIs
  • validates that configuration at both the repo level and the Collector-distribution level
  • runs a deterministic runtime harness against a patched config
  • writes durable artifacts for authors, reviewers, and CI maintainers

docs.title: Design goals
docs.text:
Meridian is built around a few explicit product priorities:

  • local-first usage
  • deterministic default behavior
  • reviewable outputs over opaque automation
  • artifact-first execution
  • concrete, Go-first implementation boundaries

These priorities shape both the user experience and the code structure.

docs.title: Intended users
docs.text:
Meridian is primarily for:

  • platform engineers who own shared Collector configurations
  • observability engineers who review telemetry routing and processing changes
  • application teams that want pre-merge confidence for Collector edits
  • CI maintainers who need a repeatable PR validation loop

docs.title: Adoption path
docs.text:
The recommended sequence is:

  1. start with validate
  2. move to check in safe mode
  3. inspect the generated artifacts
  4. add assertions or contracts where your review standard needs them
  5. wire ci into pull requests with the composite action

Meridian becomes significantly more valuable once teams rely on artifact review rather than terminal output alone.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Goals and Non-Goals
  • Users and Use Cases
  • Main Features

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian is opinionated about what it is trying to make safer. The product is centered on pre-merge confidence for OpenTelemetry Collector configuration changes.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
Meridian is designed to:

  • catch risky Collector changes before they merge
  • make Collector review easier with readable graphs, diffs, summaries, and artifacts
  • validate runtime flow without depending on live vendor backends by default
  • keep local and CI behavior close enough that results are portable
  • remain maintainable as a Go CLI with explicit orchestration stages

docs.title: Why these goals matter
docs.text:
Collector review usually fails in one of two ways:

  • teams only do static syntax checks, which are too shallow
  • teams rely on production-like environments too early, which are too noisy and expensive

Meridian tries to give teams a middle path: stronger than linting, cheaper and more deterministic than full environment testing.

docs.title: Non-goals
docs.text:
Meridian is not intended to:

  • replace production monitoring or canary analysis
  • fully validate vendor-specific auth and backend ingestion in default safe mode
  • become a general Kubernetes test framework
  • hide Collector behavior behind a proprietary control plane
  • replace expert judgment in review with a single score

docs.title: Practical implication
docs.text:
When Meridian says a run passed, the correct interpretation is:

the selected config, Collector target, and runtime harness produced the expected evidence under the chosen mode.

That is intentionally narrower than “production is safe,” and the docs should preserve that distinction everywhere.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian's command surface is compact, but each command sits in a broader review model. The right way to understand the product is by capability, not by memorizing verbs.

docs.title: Static validation
docs.text:
validate loads the config, performs repo-side validation where possible, and runs Collector-native semantic validation against a real target. It is the first fast-confidence step.

See Validate.

docs.title: Graph generation
docs.text:
graph turns Collector pipeline structure into a graph model and artifacts. It makes topology changes reviewable instead of buried in YAML.

See Graph.

docs.title: Diff-aware review
docs.text:
diff classifies changes and highlights the parts of a config edit that deserve careful review. It prefers effective config when that evidence exists.

See Diff.

docs.title: Runtime proof
docs.text:
test, check, and ci run the deterministic harness. These commands patch the config, boot a Collector, inject synthetic telemetry, wait for capture, evaluate assertions and contracts, and write an artifact bundle.

See Runtime Commands.

docs.title: Assertions and contracts
docs.text:
Assertions provide runtime flow checks. Contracts extend that into fixture-driven behavior validation against normalized telemetry.

See Assertions and Contracts.

docs.title: Artifact inspection
docs.text:
debug subcommands let engineers inspect the bundle after the fact, which matters because reviewers often consume output asynchronously.

See Debug Commands.

docs.title: CI integration
docs.text:
The composite GitHub Action turns Meridian into a PR review primitive by uploading artifacts, writing a step summary, and optionally updating a persistent PR comment.

See GitHub Action.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian serves multiple engineering roles, but they do not use the product in the same way. Good documentation should make those paths explicit.

docs.title: Platform and observability engineers
docs.text:
This is Meridian's primary audience.

Typical use cases:

  • editing shared Collector configs in Git
  • verifying route, processor, or exporter changes locally
  • producing artifacts that other reviewers can inspect
  • capturing the difference between expected negative behavior and an actual regression

These users usually care most about validate, check, diff, and artifact quality.

docs.title: Reviewers
docs.text:
Reviewers often do not run the CLI themselves. They consume the output from CI or from an artifact bundle attached to a pull request.

They care about:

  • clear risk highlights
  • graph artifacts
  • semantic validation stage results
  • runtime evidence and contract results
  • summaries that explain failure without requiring reruns

docs.title: CI maintainers
docs.text:
CI maintainers care about consistency and scriptability.

They need:

  • predictable exit codes
  • machine-readable JSON output
  • PR-comment and step-summary support
  • durable artifact uploads
  • enough context to debug failures from the workflow logs and bundle

docs.title: Meridian maintainers
docs.text: Maintainers need deeper confidence in Meridian itself. That is why the repository includes a repo-owned k3s fixture and a dedicated regression workflow outside the normal user path.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Main Features
  • Recommended Workflows

docs.title: Project
docs.text: This section is for contributors and maintainers working on Meridian itself rather than only consuming the CLI.

docs.title: Included pages
docs.text:
• Contributing
  • Docs Authoring
  • Release Docs Workflow

docs.title: Development loop
docs.text:
go test ./...
go build ./...

When command help text changes, regenerate the CLI docs:

go run ./cmd/meridian-docs

docs.title: Code ownership guidelines
docs.text:
• keep validation rules in internal/validate
  • keep capture behavior in internal/capture
  • keep telemetry generation in internal/generator
  • keep runtime orchestration in internal/runtime
  • keep reporting renderers pure where possible

docs.title: Reproducing CI failures
docs.text:
• download the artifact bundle
  • inspect report.json and collector.log
  • rerun locally with --keep-containers

docs.title: Docs Authoring
docs.text: Meridian documentation is built with MkDocs, Material for MkDocs, mike, generated Cobra reference pages, and redirect support for published URLs.

docs.title: Local workflow
docs.text:
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install -r docs/requirements.txt
go run ./cmd/meridian-docs
mkdocs serve

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• keep generated CLI pages under docs/reference/cli/ machine-generated
  • update command help text in Go code, then regenerate
  • keep hand-authored pages explanatory, not just list-based
  • preserve redirects when moving published documentation URLs

docs.title: CI expectations
docs.text:
The docs workflow:

  • regenerates CLI pages
  • fails on generated-doc drift
  • builds the site in strict mode
  • publishes versioned docs with mike

docs.title: Release Docs Workflow
docs.text: Documentation publishing is separate from the product-facing composite action.

docs.title: Branch and tag behavior
docs.text:
• pull requests build the site and fail on docs drift
  • pushes to main publish the dev docs version
  • v* tags publish <major>.<minor> and update latest

docs.title: Version policy
docs.text:
The site keeps:

  • dev
  • latest
  • the current release plus the previous four release-doc versions

docs.title: Why versioned docs matter
docs.text: Meridian's CLI behavior, artifact contract, and docs structure are part of its user interface. Versioned docs reduce drift between published binaries and the reference site.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Reference pages answer exact questions about Meridian contracts, flags, inputs, outputs, and artifacts. Start here when you already understand the product model and need details rather than explanation.

docs.title: Included reference sets
docs.text:
• CLI Overview
  • GitHub Action Reference
  • Artifact Contract
  • Config Inputs
  • Examples
  • Exit Codes
  • Generated CLI Reference

docs.title: GitHub Action Reference
docs.text: The composite action lives at action/action.yml.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text: Input Required Default Notes config yes none path to the Collector config in the checked-out workspace engine no auto auto, docker, containerd mode no safe safe, tee, live collector_image no pinned contrib image runtime Collector image timeout no 30s overall runtime timeout env_file no empty optional dotenv file assertions no empty v1 assertions or v2 contracts file render_graph no mermaid runtime graph artifact mode comment_mode no update off, create, update artifact_retention_days no 7 uploaded artifact retention

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
The action:

  • builds Meridian from source in the checked-out repository
  • runs meridian ci
  • uploads meridian-artifacts, summary.md, and report.json
  • writes the GitHub step summary
  • can update one PR comment marked with <!-- meridian-comment -->

docs.title: Permissions
docs.text:
For PR comment updates, the workflow needs:

  • contents: read
  • pull-requests: write

docs.title: Core files
docs.text:
• report.json
  • summary.md
  • config.patched.yaml
  • graph.mmd
  • collector.log
  • captures/

docs.title: Conditional files
docs.text:
• config.final.yaml
  • collector-components.json
  • semantic-findings.json
  • graph.svg
  • diff.md
  • contracts.json
  • contracts.md
  • capture.normalized.json

docs.title: Runtime metadata carried in report.json
docs.text:
report.json includes:

  • config path and runtime config source
  • engine and runtime backend
  • timings
  • findings
  • diff results
  • graph model
  • semantic report
  • test plan
  • assertions, contracts, and captures
  • artifact manifest paths

docs.title: latest symlink
docs.text: Meridian maintains runs/latest as a symlink to the most recent runtime run under the selected output root.

docs.title: K3s fixture contract
docs.text:
Per scenario, the fixture runner preserves:

  • summary.md
  • summary.json
  • logs/*.log
  • kubectl-get-all.txt
  • events.txt
  • prom-http-requests.json
  • prom-checkout.json
  • prom-heartbeat.json
  • loki-storefront.json
  • tempo-storefront.json

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Meridian's CLI is small on purpose. The commands map to a few distinct user intents rather than a large set of modes.

docs.title: Validate and understand a config
docs.text:
• validate
  • graph
  • diff

docs.title: Prove runtime flow
docs.text:
• test
  • check
  • ci

docs.title: Inspect previous runs
docs.text:
• debug logs
  • debug capture
  • debug summary
  • debug bundle

docs.title: Shell and metadata helpers
docs.text:
• version
  • completion

docs.title: Recommended entry points
docs.text:
• first local run: validate, then check
  • PR review workflow: diff, then ci
  • post-failure inspection: debug summary, debug bundle, debug logs

docs.title: Generated reference
docs.text: The generated pages under Generated CLI Reference are the source of truth for exact command help, inherited flags, and usage strings.

docs.title: Repeatable --config
docs.text:
--config accepts repeatable config sources, including local files and supported Collector-native URIs.

Example:

./bin/meridian validate \
  -c collector.yaml \
  -c yaml:exporters::otlp::endpoint=localhost:4317

docs.title: --config-dir
docs.text:
Use --config-dir when the configuration already exists as a rendered directory of YAML files.

./bin/meridian validate --config-dir ./rendered-config

docs.title: Materializable vs non-materializable sources
docs.text:
Meridian can reason repo-side about:

  • local YAML files
  • yaml: sources

Sources that cannot be materialized locally may still be valid for Collector-native semantic validation, but they reduce what Meridian can inspect without print-config.

docs.title: Env resolution
docs.text:
Env values are resolved from:

  1. --env-file
  2. repeatable --env KEY=value
  3. exported shell environment variables

docs.title: Collector target selection
docs.text:
For semantic validation, Meridian prefers:

  1. --collector-binary
  2. otherwise --collector-image through the selected engine

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: The repository ships example configs and fixtures under examples/.

docs.title: Collector config examples
docs.text:
• examples/basic/collector.yaml
  • examples/routing/collector.yaml
  • examples/redaction/collector.yaml
  • examples/multipipeline/collector.yaml

docs.title: Assertion and contract examples
docs.text:
• examples/basic/assertions.yaml
  • examples/redaction/assertions.yaml

docs.title: Fixture example
docs.text:
• examples/k3s-e2e/

Use these examples as starting points for learning Meridian's command flow and output expectations.

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
Meridian keeps exit behavior intentionally simple.

Code Meaning 0 success 1 unclassified failure 2 user input, validation, or assertion/contract loading failure 3 runtime, semantic execution, artifact, or environment failure

Use exit codes as broad classes, then inspect stderr, summary.md, report.json, and the artifact bundle for exact cause.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: These pages are generated from the Cobra command tree with go run ./cmd/meridian-docs. Do not hand-edit the generated command pages. Update command help text in Go code, then regenerate.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• meridian: Review and runtime-test OpenTelemetry Collector configs
  • meridian check: Run Meridian's opinionated end-to-end confidence workflow
  • meridian ci: CI-friendly compatibility wrapper around check
  • meridian completion: Generate shell completion scripts
  • meridian debug: Inspect artifacts from a previous Meridian run
  • meridian debug bundle: Print the run bundle manifest
  • meridian debug capture: Print capture samples from a run directory
  • meridian debug logs: Print collector logs from a run directory
  • meridian debug summary: Print the stored markdown summary
  • meridian diff: Compare two collector configs and classify risky changes
  • meridian graph: Build a pipeline graph for the collector config
  • meridian test: Run the runtime harness against the collector config
  • meridian validate: Run static validation against a collector config
  • meridian version: Print the Meridian version

docs.title: meridian
docs.text: Review and runtime-test OpenTelemetry Collector configs

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--collector-binary string   path to a collector binary used for semantic validation
  -c, --config stringArray        collector config source; repeatable and may be a file path or collector config URI
      --config-dir string         path to a rendered collector config directory
      --env stringArray           inline KEY=VALUE env vars
      --env-file string           dotenv file used for config interpolation
      --format string             output format: human|json (default "human")
  -h, --help                      help for meridian
      --quiet                     suppress human progress output
      --verbose                   enable verbose output

docs.title: SEE ALSO
docs.text:
• meridian check - Run Meridian's opinionated end-to-end confidence workflow
  • meridian ci - CI-friendly compatibility wrapper around check
  • meridian completion - Generate shell completion scripts
  • meridian debug - Inspect artifacts from a previous Meridian run
  • meridian diff - Compare two collector configs and classify risky changes
  • meridian graph - Build a pipeline graph for the collector config
  • meridian test - Run the runtime harness against the collector config
  • meridian validate - Run static validation against a collector config
  • meridian version - Print the Meridian version

docs.title: meridian check
docs.text:
Run Meridian's opinionated end-to-end confidence workflow

meridian check [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--assertions string           assertions or contracts YAML file
      --base string                 git base ref used to materialize the old config
      --capture-samples int         maximum captured telemetry samples to persist per signal (default 5)
      --capture-timeout duration    capture wait timeout (default 10s)
      --changed-only                require explicit diff inputs and include only diff-aware review hints
      --collector-image string      collector image to run (default "otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib@sha256:e7c92c715f28ff142f3bcaccd4fc5603cf4c71276ef09954a38eb4038500a5a5")
      --engine string               container engine: auto|docker|containerd (default "auto")
      --head string                 git head ref used to materialize the new config
  -h, --help                        help for check
      --inject-timeout duration     telemetry injection timeout (default 5s)
      --keep-containers             keep the collector container running after the test
      --mode string                 runtime mode: safe|tee|live (default "safe")
      --new string                  new collector config file used for diff-aware review
      --old string                  old collector config file used for diff-aware review
      --output string               artifact output directory (default "./meridian-artifacts")
      --pipelines strings           limit runtime checks to specific signals or pipelines
      --render-graph string         additional graph artifact for runtime commands: mermaid|svg|none (default "mermaid")
      --seed int                    deterministic generation seed (default 42)
      --severity-threshold string   minimum diff severity threshold: low|medium|high (default "low")
      --startup-timeout duration    collector startup timeout (default 10s)
      --timeout duration            overall runtime timeout (default 30s)

docs.title: Options inherited from parent commands
docs.text:
--collector-binary string   path to a collector binary used for semantic validation
  -c, --config stringArray        collector config source; repeatable and may be a file path or collector config URI
      --config-dir string         path to a rendered collector config directory
      --env stringArray           inline KEY=VALUE env vars
      --env-file string           dotenv file used for config interpolation
      --format string             output format: human|json (default "human")
      --quiet                     suppress human progress output
      --verbose                   enable verbose output

docs.title: SEE ALSO

docs.title: meridian ci
docs.text:
CI-friendly compatibility wrapper around check

meridian ci [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--assertions string           assertions or contracts YAML file
      --base string                 git base ref used to materialize the old config
      --capture-samples int         maximum captured telemetry samples to persist per signal (default 5)
      --capture-timeout duration    capture wait timeout (default 10s)
      --changed-only                require explicit diff inputs and include only diff-aware review hints

      --engine string               container engine: auto|docker|containerd (default "auto")
      --head string                 git head ref used to materialize the new config
  -h, --help                        help for ci
      --inject-timeout duration     telemetry injection timeout (default 5s)
      --json-file string            write the JSON report to a file
      --keep-containers             keep the collector container running after the test
      --mode string                 runtime mode: safe|tee|live (default "safe")
      --new string                  new collector config file used for diff-aware review
      --old string                  old collector config file used for diff-aware review

docs.title: Recommended Workflows
docs.text:
Meridian is most effective when engineers use it as part of a repeatable workflow, not as an ad hoc command runner.

This section documents the operating patterns the product is designed around:

  • local config authoring
  • reviewer-oriented pull request validation
  • CI maintenance
  • Meridian maintainer regression work

Choose the path that matches your role, then treat the related artifacts as part of the workflow contract.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This workflow is for the engineer who owns the pull request automation rather than the Collector config itself.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Add the Meridian action to the workflow.
  2. select the config path, runtime engine, and optional assertions file
  3. ensure the job has pull-requests: write when PR comments are desired
  4. verify artifacts, step summary, and PR comment behavior in a test pull request
  5. teach reviewers where Meridian evidence lives

docs.title: What matters operationally
docs.text:
CI maintainers should care most about:

  • deterministic action behavior
  • machine-readable ci output
  • artifact retention
  • permissions for PR comment updates
  • runner compatibility with the selected engine

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• using containerd on a runner that does not have nerdctl
  • treating the PR comment as the only evidence surface
  • ignoring uploaded artifacts when the summary is insufficient

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• GitHub Action
  • GitHub Action Reference

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This is the recommended workflow for an engineer changing a Collector config locally before opening a pull request.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Edit the config.
  2. Run meridian validate.
  3. Run meridian check.
  4. Inspect summary.md, report.json, config.patched.yaml, graph artifacts, and any contract output.
  5. If behavior changed intentionally, update assertions or contracts with care.
  6. Open the pull request with the local evidence already understood.

docs.title: Why this workflow is recommended
docs.text:
It front-loads the cheapest and most deterministic checks:

  • static structure
  • Collector-native semantics
  • runtime flow in safe mode

That usually catches the most important regressions before CI runs.

docs.title: When to deviate
docs.text: Use tee or live only when you explicitly need destination behavior beyond the default deterministic path.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Runtime Commands
  • Assertions and Contracts

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This workflow is for maintainers validating Meridian itself, especially changes to runtime orchestration, patching, reporting, and fixture evaluation.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. run go test ./...
  2. exercise the normal local CLI flows where relevant
  3. run the repo-owned k3s fixture on the project VM
  4. inspect summary.md, summary.json, and supporting scenario artifacts
  5. confirm that negative scenarios pass for the right reasons

docs.title: Why this workflow exists
docs.text: The k3s fixture is a regression target for Meridian, not a generic user tutorial. It exists to provide one controlled cluster-level acceptance surface the project owns.

docs.title: What to verify
docs.text:
Maintainers should verify:

  • scenario exit semantics
  • artifact completeness
  • fidelity of summary reporting
  • run-scoped evidence behavior for gateway counters, Prometheus, and logs

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• K3s Fixture
  • CI and Docs Publishing

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This workflow is for engineers reviewing a Collector change in a pull request, whether or not they ran Meridian locally themselves.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Read the PR diff and identify the targeted config.
  2. Read the Meridian step summary.
  3. Read the PR comment if comment mode is enabled.
  4. Inspect the uploaded artifact bundle when the summary alone is not enough.
  5. Focus on diff highlights, semantic stage status, runtime signal status, and contract failures.

docs.title: What to prioritize
docs.text:
Reviewers should pay particular attention to:

  • risk highlights in the diff section
  • whether effective-config evidence was available
  • runtime failures by signal
  • contract failures that indicate changed output shape
  • collector.log when startup or patching looks suspicious

docs.title: Failure interpretation
docs.text:
A failed run is not always a product regression. It may represent:

  • a genuine config problem
  • a missing environment dependency
  • an unsupported Collector behavior
  • a changed contract that needs explicit review

The artifact bundle should let you distinguish those cases.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Diff
  • Debug Commands
